topics.title: Amazon Route 53 — DNS and Traffic Routing
topics.description: Route 53 is AWS's scalable DNS service. It translates human-readable domain names (api.example.com) into IP addresses, routes traffic across global infrastructure using intelligent policies, and provides health checks that automatically redirect traffic away from failing endpoints.
topics.story: Imagine you're sending a letter to 'The CEO of Acme Corp'. You don't know the CEO's home address — you go to the company directory (DNS) which looks up 'CEO of Acme Corp' and returns '123 Office Street'. Route 53 is that directory. But unlike a static directory, Route 53's directory can say 'route 70% of traffic to the New York office and 30% to the Tokyo office' (weighted routing) or 'if New York is down, automatically send everything to Chicago' (failover routing).
topics.analogy: Hosted Zone = your domain's address book (all DNS records for example.com). A Record = direct address entry (example.com → 1.2.3.4). CNAME = alias entry ('shop' redirects to 'store.example.com'). Alias Record = AWS-specific CNAME for AWS resources (points to ALB/CloudFront DNS name — free queries, no extra hop). TTL = how long browsers/resolvers cache the answer before asking again. Health Check = a Route 53 agent that pings your endpoint every 30s and marks it UP or DOWN.
topics.memoryAnchor: Route 53's name: TCP/UDP port 53 is the DNS port. 'Route' because it does intelligent routing beyond plain DNS. Key record types: A (IPv4), AAAA (IPv6), CNAME (alias, cannot be at root domain), Alias (AWS extension of CNAME, CAN be at root domain). Routing policies: Simple, Weighted, Latency-based, Failover, Geolocation, Geoproximity, Multi-value. Health check interval: 30s standard, 10s fast (higher cost).
topics.firstPrinciples: DNS is a distributed, hierarchical, cached naming system. Resolution chain: browser checks local cache → OS cache → resolver (ISP) cache → Root nameserver (which TLD?) → TLD nameserver (.com, who manages example.com?) → Authoritative nameserver (Route 53 answers: example.com = 1.2.3.4). TTL controls cache lifetime at each hop. Route 53's traffic routing policies are implemented at the authoritative nameserver level — when a query arrives, Route 53 evaluates the routing policy and health checks, then returns the appropriate IP. Failover routing: Route 53 polls your endpoint, and if health checks fail, stops returning that IP in DNS responses within one TTL cycle.
topics.bruteForce: Without DNS: users must type IP addresses directly (1.2.3.4 instead of example.com). Without health-check-based routing: if your server IP goes down, DNS still returns that IP and users see errors until you manually update the DNS record (can take minutes to hours depending on TTL). Without latency routing: European users connect to your US server with 150ms extra RTT even though you have a European server.
topics.optimizedApproach: Use Alias records (not CNAME) for AWS resources — free, no extra DNS hop, works at root domain (CNAME cannot be at apex). Set low TTL (60s) for records you might need to change quickly (failover, deployments); high TTL (86400s = 24h) for stable records to reduce DNS query costs. Use latency-based routing for multi-region apps — Route 53 measures AWS region latencies from the resolver's location and routes to the lowest-latency region. Combine with health checks for automatic failover (if latency-based primary fails, Route 53 falls back to other regions). Use Route 53 Resolver for hybrid DNS (on-premise ↔ AWS VPC name resolution).
topics.whenToUse: Route 53: registering domains, hosting DNS for any AWS or non-AWS infrastructure, multi-region routing and failover, blue/green deployments (shift traffic via weighted routing), geolocation-based content serving. NOT Route 53: CDN (use CloudFront), load balancing within a region (use ALB), in-VPC service discovery (use AWS Cloud Map or ECS service discovery).

examples.title: Hosted Zones, Record Types, and Alias Records
examples.explanation: A hosted zone is a container for DNS records within a domain. After creating a hosted zone, Route 53 assigns 4 NS (nameserver) records — you update your domain registrar to point to these servers so Route 53 becomes authoritative. UPSERT = create if missing, update if exists. The Alias record is an AWS extension that acts like a CNAME but resolves at Route 53's level (no extra DNS hop), works at the root domain (apex), and queries are free. EvaluateTargetHealth on an Alias record means Route 53 monitors the ALB's own health checks — if all ALB targets are unhealthy, Route 53 stops returning that IP.
examples.pseudocode:
DNS resolution for api.example.com:
1. Client queries local resolver cache → MISS
2. Resolver queries Root nameserver: who handles .com?
   → Response: TLD nameserver for .com at 192.5.6.30
3. Resolver queries TLD (.com) nameserver: who handles example.com?
   → Response: Route 53 NS records (ns-123.awsdns-12.com etc.)
4. Resolver queries Route 53 authoritative nameserver:
   → Route 53 evaluates routing policy for api.example.com:
     a. Is there a health check? → Is endpoint healthy? YES
     b. Routing policy: SIMPLE → return 203.0.113.42
   → Response: A record, IP=203.0.113.42, TTL=300
5. Resolver returns 203.0.113.42 to client, caches for 300s
6. Client connects to 203.0.113.42
examples.realWorldUse: SaaS app: example.com Alias → CloudFront distribution (root domain, free queries). api.example.com A record → ALB. admin.example.com CNAME → internal admin portal. Blue/green deployment: api.example.com weighted records, 90% → blue ALB, 10% → green ALB, gradually shift to 100% green then retire blue.
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  Client[User Browser] -->|DNS lookup: example.com| Resolver[ISP Resolver]
  Resolver -->|cache miss| Root[Root NS]
  Root --> TLD[.com TLD NS]
  TLD --> R53[Route 53 Authoritative NS]
  R53 -->|Alias: example.com| ALB[ALB DNS name]
  R53 -->|A: api.example.com| IP[203.0.113.42]
  R53 -->|EvaluateTargetHealth| HC[ALB Health Check]
  HC -->|unhealthy| R53No[Route 53: SERVFAIL or fallback]
  HC -->|healthy| Return[Return ALB IP to resolver]
examples.tracerSteps: [{"step":1,"description":"User types example.com in browser","state":"Browser checks local DNS cache → MISS"},{"step":2,"description":"ISP resolver performs iterative resolution","state":"Root NS → .com TLD NS → Route 53 authoritative NS identified"},{"step":3,"description":"Route 53 evaluates Alias record for example.com","state":"Alias target: my-alb-1234.us-east-1.elb.amazonaws.com | EvaluateTargetHealth=true"},{"step":4,"description":"Route 53 checks ALB health status","state":"ALB health check: 3/3 targets healthy → return ALB IP"},{"step":5,"description":"Route 53 returns IP of ALB to resolver, resolver caches for TTL=60s","state":"Client connects to ALB → HTTPS → React app loads"},{"step":6,"description":"All ALB targets become unhealthy (deployment in progress)","state":"EvaluateTargetHealth: Route 53 removes this record from responses → clients get failover record (if configured) or SERVFAIL"}]

examples.title: Routing Policies — Weighted, Latency, and Failover
examples.explanation: Weighted routing splits traffic by proportion (90+10=100, but weights are relative ratios — 9+1 works the same). Low TTL (60s) is critical for canary releases so clients pick up the traffic shift quickly. Latency-based routing uses AWS's internal network latency measurements between Route 53 edge servers (co-located with resolvers) and the specified AWS regions — not the actual application latency, but highly correlated. Health checks on latency records: if the nearest region is unhealthy, Route 53 automatically routes to the next-lowest-latency healthy region. Failover routing is an active/passive disaster recovery pattern — primary handles all traffic until its health check fails, then secondary takes over within one TTL cycle.
examples.pseudocode:
Latency-based routing evaluation:
  Query arrives from Mumbai ISP resolver (89.97.168.1)
  Route 53 evaluates latency records for api.example.com:
    - Record: us-east-1 (54.1.2.3) health=UP
      → measured latency from resolver to us-east-1: ~180ms
    - Record: ap-south-1 (13.4.5.6) health=UP
      → measured latency from resolver to ap-south-1: ~12ms
  → Return ap-south-1 record (lowest latency)
  
  Same query, ap-south-1 health=DOWN:
    → ap-south-1 excluded
    → Only us-east-1 remains → return 54.1.2.3
  
Weighted routing evaluation (weight 90 + weight 10):
  Random number 0-99:
    0-89 (90%) → return blue record
    90-99 (10%) → return green record
examples.realWorldUse: Canary deployment: deploy new API version to 10% of traffic, monitor error rates in CloudWatch, gradually increase to 100% over 30 minutes. Multi-region active-active: Route 53 latency routing with health checks — Indian users → Mumbai region, US users → US-East region, each with automatic failover. DR drill: set primary health check to fail manually → verify all traffic moves to secondary within 60s (one TTL cycle) → re-enable primary → traffic returns.
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  subgraph Weighted
    QW[DNS query] --> R53W[Route 53]
    R53W -->|90%| Blue[Blue v1: 10.0.1.10]
    R53W -->|10%| Green[Green v2: 10.0.2.10]
  end
  subgraph Latency
    QL[Indian user DNS] --> R53L[Route 53]
    R53L -->|12ms| Mumbai[ap-south-1: 13.4.5.6]
    R53L -->|180ms| US[us-east-1: 54.1.2.3]
    Mumbai -->|health check FAIL| R53L
    R53L -->|fallback| US
  end
  subgraph Failover
    QF[DNS query] --> R53F[Route 53]
    R53F -->|primary UP| Primary[us-east-1]
    R53F -->|primary DOWN| Secondary[eu-west-1 DR]
  end
examples.tracerSteps: [{"step":1,"description":"Canary release: deploy v2 to green server, set Route 53 weight 90/10","state":"Blue v1 weight=90, Green v2 weight=10, TTL=60s"},{"step":2,"description":"10% of DNS resolutions return green IP (13.4.5.6)","state":"CloudWatch: error rate green=0.1%, blue=0.1% — v2 looks healthy"},{"step":3,"description":"Gradually shift: blue weight=50, green weight=50","state":"50/50 split — monitoring for 10 minutes"},{"step":4,"description":"Blue weight=0, green weight=100 — v2 fully deployed","state":"All new DNS resolutions return green IP. Old cached blue IPs expire after TTL=60s"},{"step":5,"description":"Indian user: DNS query for api.example.com","state":"Route 53 latency check: Mumbai(12ms) < US(180ms) → return Mumbai IP"},{"step":6,"description":"Mumbai region becomes unavailable","state":"Health check fails after 3 consecutive failures (30s apart) → Route 53 removes Mumbai → returns US IP"}]

problems.title: Configure Multi-Region Failover with Health Checks
problems.description: Your app runs in us-east-1 (primary) and eu-west-1 (DR). Design the Route 53 configuration: (1) Primary must fail over to DR if HTTP health check to /health returns non-200 for 3 consecutive checks. (2) Recovery must be automatic when primary comes back up. (3) TTL must allow failover within 90 seconds.
constraints: Health check interval: 30s standard
3 failures × 30s = 90s to declare unhealthy
TTL must be ≤ 90s for failover to complete within 90s

examples.output: Failover=PRIMARY record with HealthCheckId on 54.1.2.3, TTL=60s | Failover=SECONDARY on 52.6.7.8 (no health check needed on secondary). Health check: HTTP, /health, port 443, failureThreshold=3, requestInterval=30
examples.explanation: SECONDARY has no health check — it's the last resort. If it also had a health check and was unhealthy, Route 53 would have no record to return

hints: HealthCheckId attached to PRIMARY record only
Failover time = (failureThreshold × requestInterval) + TTL = (3×30) + 60 = 150s worst case
For 90s guarantee: use Fast health check (10s interval), failureThreshold=3 → 30s + TTL=60s = 90s
problems.sampleOutput: Failover=PRIMARY record with HealthCheckId on 54.1.2.3, TTL=60s | Failover=SECONDARY on 52.6.7.8 (no health check needed on secondary). Health check: HTTP, /health, port 443, failureThreshold=3, requestInterval=30

problems.title: Canary Deployment with Route 53 Weighted Routing
problems.description: You're deploying a new API version to 5% of traffic. After 10 minutes if error rate < 0.1%, increase to 25%, then 50%, then 100%. What Route 53 configuration enables this, and what CloudWatch metric should trigger a rollback?
constraints: Two records: same Name, same Type, different SetIdentifier
Weights are relative (90+10 = same as 9+1 = same as 900+100)
TTL=60s so traffic shifts take effect within 1 minute

examples.output: Route 53: two A records with SetIdentifier='v1'/'v2'. Rollback: CloudWatch Alarm on HTTP 5xx rate > 0.5% on v2 ALB → Lambda updates Route 53 weight v2=0
examples.explanation: Weight=0 effectively removes a record from rotation — all traffic goes to v1

hints: aws route53 change-resource-record-sets with Weight=0 to remove a record from rotation
CloudWatch → Route 53 automated rollback: EventBridge + Lambda watches the alarm
SetIdentifier must be unique within the same Name + Type combination
problems.sampleOutput: Route 53: two A records with SetIdentifier='v1'/'v2'. Rollback: CloudWatch Alarm on HTTP 5xx rate > 0.5% on v2 ALB → Lambda updates Route 53 weight v2=0

problems.title: CNAME vs Alias Record
problems.description: A developer set up 'example.com CNAME my-alb.amazonaws.com' and it doesn't work. They also notice they're being charged extra DNS query fees. Explain why CNAME at root domain fails, what the correct record type is, and why it costs less.
constraints: Root domain = apex = the bare domain with no subdomain prefix
RFC 1034 prohibits CNAME at apex alongside other records (SOA, NS)
Alias record is an AWS Route 53 extension, not standard DNS

examples.output: Fix: example.com Alias A record → AliasTarget.DNSName=my-alb.amazonaws.com. Benefit: free queries (CNAME billed per query), works at apex, EvaluateTargetHealth available
examples.explanation: Alias records are resolved by Route 53 before returning to the client — no extra DNS hop, no extra billing

hints: CNAME cannot coexist with SOA/NS records at the root — violates RFC 1034
Alias record Type is A (or AAAA) but with an AliasTarget instead of ResourceRecords
EvaluateTargetHealth only works with Alias records, not CNAME
problems.sampleOutput: Fix: example.com Alias A record → AliasTarget.DNSName=my-alb.amazonaws.com. Benefit: free queries (CNAME billed per query), works at apex, EvaluateTargetHealth available

topics.title: Amazon CloudFront — CDN and Edge Caching
topics.description: CloudFront is AWS's Content Delivery Network (CDN). It caches your content at 400+ edge locations worldwide, serving requests from the nearest edge node instead of your origin server — reducing latency from 200ms to under 10ms for cached content, while slashing origin server load.
topics.story: Imagine your bakery is in New York, but you have customers in Tokyo, London, and São Paulo. Without CloudFront, every customer drives to New York to pick up bread — hours of travel. With CloudFront, you ship bread to local partner bakeries (edge locations) in every city. Tokyo customers get Tokyo bread (cached copy). When the recipe changes (new deployment), you call all partner bakeries and tell them to refresh their stock (cache invalidation). Your New York bakery (origin) only makes bread when a local bakery runs out (cache miss).
topics.analogy: Distribution = your CloudFront deployment (one global CDN setup per origin). Origin = where CloudFront fetches content from on cache miss (S3 bucket, ALB, custom HTTP). Edge Location = the 400+ PoPs (Points of Presence) where content is cached globally. Cache Behavior = rules for how to handle specific URL patterns (which headers to cache by, TTL, compression). TTL = how long the edge holds a cached copy before re-fetching from origin. Cache Hit Ratio = % of requests served from cache (higher = better, less origin load). Invalidation = forcing all edges to delete cached copies (triggers fresh origin fetch).
topics.memoryAnchor: CloudFront key numbers: 400+ edge locations. Default TTL = 86400s (24h). Max file size = 20GB. HTTPS: CloudFront terminates TLS at edge (origin connection can be HTTP internally). Price classes: Price Class All (all edges, lowest latency, highest cost), Price Class 100 (US+Europe only), Price Class 200 (adds Asia, Middle East). Free tier: 1TB transfer out + 10M HTTP requests/month for 12 months.
topics.firstPrinciples: A CDN works by placing caches geographically close to users. CloudFront uses the concept of a 'cache key' — a fingerprint of the request that determines whether a cached copy can be served. By default, the cache key is the URL + Host header. You can add query strings, cookies, or custom headers to the cache key (but this fragments the cache — more unique keys = lower hit ratio). CloudFront's origin shield is an optional additional caching layer in front of your origin — consolidates cache misses from all 400+ edges into one region, reducing origin load by 60-80%. CloudFront is also a WAF integration point — all traffic passes through the edge before reaching your origin, enabling edge-level security.
topics.bruteForce: Without CloudFront, a static React app served from S3 in us-east-1: Tokyo user fetches 500KB bundle.js across the Pacific — 150ms RTT × multiple round trips = ~600ms total load time. With CloudFront edge in Tokyo: bundle.js cached at edge after first request → ~8ms RTT → subsequent requests < 10ms. S3 origin transfer cost: $0.09/GB. CloudFront transfer cost: $0.08/GB (edge) + $0.004/GB (origin to CloudFront) = $0.084/GB AND reduces origin requests by 95%.
topics.optimizedApproach: Maximize cache hit ratio: keep cache key minimal (URL only), use Cache-Control headers from origin (max-age directive), separate static assets (long TTL, versioned filenames) from HTML (short TTL or no cache). Use versioned file names (bundle.abc123.js) instead of invalidations — a new deploy creates new URLs, old URLs naturally expire. Reserve invalidations for emergencies (index.html). Enable Gzip/Brotli compression at CloudFront edge — reduce transfer size by 60-80%. Use CloudFront Functions (sub-millisecond, lightweight JS) for URL rewrites, redirects, and header manipulation. Use Lambda@Edge for heavier computation at the edge (A/B testing, auth, personalisation).
topics.whenToUse: CloudFront: static website hosting (S3 + CloudFront), API acceleration (caching GET responses), DDoS protection (absorbs traffic at edge), HTTPS termination, streaming video (HLS/DASH), software distribution. NOT CloudFront: WebSocket real-time connections (use API Gateway WebSocket or ALB), content that's highly personalised per user (low cache hit ratio → defeats the purpose), data that changes faster than TTL allows.

examples.title: CloudFront Distribution for S3 Static Site and ALB API
examples.code:
# CloudFormation template: CloudFront with two origins
# - S3 origin for static React frontend
# - ALB origin for Spring Boot API
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'

Resources:
  CloudFrontDistribution:
    Type: AWS::CloudFront::Distribution
    Properties:
      DistributionConfig:
        Enabled: true
        HttpVersion: http2
        DefaultRootObject: index.html
        PriceClass: PriceClass_All
        
        # Custom domain + HTTPS certificate
        Aliases:
          - example.com
          - www.example.com
        ViewerCertificate:
          AcmCertificateArn: !Sub 'arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:${AWS::AccountId}:certificate/abc-123'
          SslSupportMethod: sni-only
          MinimumProtocolVersion: TLSv1.2_2021
        
        Origins:
          # S3 origin for static files (private bucket)
          - Id: S3Origin
            DomainName: !GetAtt FrontendBucket.RegionalDomainName
            S3OriginConfig:
              OriginAccessIdentity: !Sub 'origin-access-identity/cloudfront/${OAI}'
          
          # ALB origin for API
          - Id: ALBOrigin
            DomainName: my-alb-1234.us-east-1.elb.amazonaws.com
            CustomOriginConfig:
              HTTPSPort: 443
              OriginProtocolPolicy: https-only
              OriginSSLProtocols:
                - TLSv1.2
        
        CacheBehaviors:
          # API calls: bypass cache entirely
          - PathPattern: /api/*
            TargetOriginId: ALBOrigin
            ViewerProtocolPolicy: redirect-to-https
            CachePolicyId: 4135ea2d-6df8-44a3-9df3-4b5a84be39ad  # CachingDisabled
            OriginRequestPolicyId: b689b0a8-53d0-40ab-baf2-68738e2966ac  # AllViewer
            AllowedMethods: [GET, HEAD, OPTIONS, PUT, POST, PATCH, DELETE]
        
        # Default behavior: serve from S3 (React app)
        DefaultCacheBehavior:
          TargetOriginId: S3Origin
          ViewerProtocolPolicy: redirect-to-https
          CachePolicyId: 658327ea-f89d-4fab-a63d-7e88639e58f6  # CachingOptimized
          Compress: true  # Gzip/Brotli at edge
          AllowedMethods: [GET, HEAD]
        
        # SPA routing fix: 403/404 from S3 → serve index.html
        # React Router handles the route client-side
        CustomErrorResponses:
          - ErrorCode: 403
            ResponsePagePath: /index.html
            ResponseCode: 200
          - ErrorCode: 404
            ResponsePagePath: /index.html
            ResponseCode: 200

  # Origin Access Identity: CloudFront → S3 (S3 bucket stays private)
  OAI:
    Type: AWS::CloudFront::CloudFrontOriginAccessIdentity
    Properties:
      CloudFrontOriginAccessIdentityConfig:
        Comment: 'OAI for frontend bucket'

  FrontendBucket:
    Type: AWS::S3::Bucket
    Properties:
      # PublicAccessBlockConfiguration: all blocked (private bucket)
      PublicAccessBlockConfiguration:
        BlockPublicAcls: true
        BlockPublicPolicy: true
examples.explanation: Two origins, two behaviors: /api/* requests bypass the cache (CachingDisabled policy) and forward to the ALB, passing all request headers/cookies. Everything else (static assets) is served from S3 with aggressive caching. Origin Access Identity (OAI) is a CloudFront identity that the S3 bucket policy allows — keeps the S3 bucket private (not publicly accessible), only CloudFront can fetch from it. The CustomErrorResponses block is essential for single-page applications — React Router handles routes like /dashboard client-side, but if a user directly navigates to /dashboard, CloudFront fetches /dashboard from S3, gets a 403 (file not found), and this rule rewrites it to serve index.html with a 200 status, letting React Router take over.
examples.pseudocode:
CloudFront request evaluation:
  Incoming request: GET https://example.com/api/users/me
  
  1. Find matching cache behavior:
     /api/*  → matches → ALBOrigin, CachingDisabled
  
  2. CachingDisabled → skip cache → forward to ALB origin:
     GET https://my-alb.amazonaws.com/api/users/me
     + all viewer headers forwarded (OriginRequestPolicy=AllViewer)
  
  3. ALB processes, returns JSON
  4. CloudFront forwards response to viewer (not cached)
  
  Incoming request: GET https://example.com/assets/bundle.abc123.js
  
  1. No matching path pattern → DefaultCacheBehavior → S3Origin, CachingOptimized
  2. Cache check: hash(url) → edge cache → MISS (first request)
  3. Forward to S3: GET s3://frontend-bucket/assets/bundle.abc123.js
  4. S3 returns file with Cache-Control: max-age=31536000
  5. CloudFront caches at edge for 31536000s (1 year — versioned filename)
  6. Next request: MISS → HIT from edge cache → served in <5ms
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner frontend: S3 bucket (private) + CloudFront + OAI. React build output: bundle.[contenthash].js cached for 1 year. index.html: Cache-Control: no-cache (always fresh). Custom domain example.com: Route 53 Alias → CloudFront distribution. API calls (/api/*): CloudFront forwards to ALB (no caching — user data). DDoS: AWS Shield Standard (free) protects CloudFront against volumetric attacks at edge.
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  User -->|GET example.com/dashboard| CF[CloudFront Edge]
  CF --> CB{Cache Behavior?}
  CB -->|/api/*| ALB[ALB Origin]
  CB -->|default /| S3O[S3 Origin]
  S3O --> CacheCheck{Edge Cache?}
  CacheCheck -->|HIT| Fast[<5ms response]
  CacheCheck -->|MISS| S3[Fetch from S3]
  S3 -->|Cache-Control: max-age=31536000| CacheStore[Cache at edge]
  CacheStore --> Fast
  ALB -->|JSON response| User
  CF --> SPA[GET /dashboard → 403 from S3]
  SPA -->|CustomErrorResponse| Index[Serve index.html with 200]
examples.tracerSteps: [{"step":1,"description":"User navigates to example.com — CloudFront edge in user's city handles request","state":"GET / → matches DefaultCacheBehavior → S3Origin"},{"step":2,"description":"Edge cache check for /index.html","state":"Cache MISS (index.html has no-cache header) → fetch from S3"},{"step":3,"description":"S3 returns index.html (2KB) + Cache-Control: no-cache","state":"CloudFront does NOT cache. Returns to user. React app boots."},{"step":4,"description":"React app fetches /assets/bundle.abc123.js","state":"Edge cache MISS (first request) → fetch from S3"},{"step":5,"description":"S3 returns bundle.js (450KB) + Cache-Control: max-age=31536000","state":"CloudFront caches at edge for 1 year. Served in 180ms (origin fetch)."},{"step":6,"description":"Second user in same city requests /assets/bundle.abc123.js","state":"Edge cache HIT — served in 4ms, no S3 request"},{"step":7,"description":"User navigates directly to /profile — S3 returns 403 (no /profile file)","state":"CustomErrorResponse: 403 → /index.html with 200. React Router renders /profile page."}]

examples.title: Cache Invalidation, Lambda@Edge, and Signed URLs
examples.code:
import boto3
import time
import hashlib
import json
from datetime import datetime, timezone

cf_client = boto3.client('cloudfront')

# ── Cache Invalidation — force all edges to re-fetch ──────────────────
def invalidate_cache(distribution_id, paths):
    """
    Use sparingly — each invalidation path is billed after 1000 free/month.
    Best practice: use versioned filenames instead.
    Invalidate only index.html (the entry point) on deployment.
    """
    response = cf_client.create_invalidation(
        DistributionId=distribution_id,
        InvalidationBatch={
            'Paths': {
                'Quantity': len(paths),
                'Items': paths
            },
            'CallerReference': str(int(time.time()))  # Unique ID for this invalidation
        }
    )
    invalidation_id = response['Invalidation']['Id']
    print(f"Invalidation created: {invalidation_id}")
    # Invalidation takes 30-60 seconds to propagate to all 400+ edges
    return invalidation_id

# Deployment script: only invalidate the HTML entry point
def deploy_frontend(distribution_id):
    # 1. Upload new build to S3 (hashed filenames — old files remain)
    # aws s3 sync dist/ s3://my-bucket/ --delete
    
    # 2. Only invalidate index.html (the bootstrap file)
    # New bundle.abc123.js has a new hash → new URL → no invalidation needed
    invalidate_cache(distribution_id, ['/index.html'])
    print("Deployment complete. index.html invalidated — hashed assets auto-expire by URL.")

# ── Signed URLs — time-limited private content access ─────────────────
# Use case: paid content, private downloads, expiring share links
def generate_signed_url(cloudfront_domain, object_key, key_pair_id, private_key_pem, expire_seconds=3600):
    """
    CloudFront Signed URL: access a private file for a limited time.
    Requires: key pair created in CloudFront console, public key associated with distribution.
    private_key_pem: the private key file contents (RSA 2048-bit)
    """
    from cryptography.hazmat.primitives import hashes, serialization
    from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric import padding
    import base64
    
    expire_epoch = int(time.time()) + expire_seconds
    url = f"https://{cloudfront_domain}/{object_key}"
    
    # Policy document
    policy = json.dumps({
        'Statement': [{
            'Resource': url,
            'Condition': {'DateLessThan': {'AWS:EpochTime': expire_epoch}}
        }]
    }, separators=(',', ':'))
    
    # Sign the policy with private key
    private_key = serialization.load_pem_private_key(private_key_pem.encode(), password=None)
    signature = private_key.sign(policy.encode(), padding.PKCS1v15(), hashes.SHA1())
    
    encoded_policy = base64.b64encode(policy.encode()).decode().replace('+','-').replace('=','_').replace('/','~')
    encoded_sig = base64.b64encode(signature).decode().replace('+','-').replace('=','_').replace('/','~')
    
    return f"{url}?Policy={encoded_policy}&Signature={encoded_sig}&Key-Pair-Id={key_pair_id}"

# ── Lambda@Edge — execute logic at the edge ────────────────────────────
# Deployed to us-east-1, replicated to ALL CloudFront edges automatically
# Triggers: viewer-request, origin-request, origin-response, viewer-response

def lambda_at_edge_handler(event, context):
    """
    Example: viewer-request trigger
    Use case: A/B testing — route 50% of users to v2 based on cookie
    """
    request = event['Records'][0]['cf']['request']
    headers = request['headers']
    
    # Check if user has an A/B test cookie
    cookies = headers.get('cookie', [{}])[0].get('value', '')
    cookie_dict = dict(c.strip().split('=', 1) for c in cookies.split(';') if '=' in c)
    
    ab_group = cookie_dict.get('ab_group')
    if not ab_group:
        # Assign group deterministically by hashing the CloudFront viewer IP
        viewer_ip = event['Records'][0]['cf']['config'].get('requestId', '')
        ab_group = 'v2' if int(hashlib.md5(viewer_ip.encode()).hexdigest(), 16) % 2 == 0 else 'v1'
    
    # Rewrite origin path based on A/B group
    if ab_group == 'v2':
        request['uri'] = request['uri'].replace('/assets/', '/assets-v2/')
    
    # Add header so origin knows the group for analytics
    headers['x-ab-group'] = [{'key': 'X-AB-Group', 'value': ab_group}]
    
    return request  # Modified request forwarded to origin
examples.explanation: Cache invalidation is a blunt tool — it costs money (after 1000 free paths/month) and takes 30-60s to propagate. The professional approach is versioned filenames: webpack/Vite automatically generate hashes in filenames (bundle.abc123.js). When you deploy, new files have new URLs — old cached copies naturally expire by TTL. Only index.html needs invalidation (it's not versioned and contains the links to new hashed assets). Signed URLs enable time-limited access to private CloudFront content — perfect for paid content or expiring share links. Lambda@Edge runs at the edge point of presence itself, adding compute without an origin round-trip — used for auth, A/B testing, header manipulation, URL rewrites at sub-millisecond latency.
examples.pseudocode:
Versioned deployment strategy:
  Build output:
    index.html          (no hash — always current version)
    assets/main.abc123.js (hash changes with every build)
    assets/main.def456.css
  
  Deploy:
    s3 sync → upload ALL files (new hashed names + new index.html)
    Invalidate: ["/index.html"] only
    Old hashed files: still cached at edges until TTL expires
    New hashed files: cache miss on first request → fetched from S3
    index.html: invalidated → fresh fetch → references new hashed filenames
  
  Net effect:
    Zero invalidation cost for JS/CSS (new URLs)
    1 invalidation path used (/index.html)
    No 404s for users who cached old index.html (old hashed files still in S3)
examples.realWorldUse: Video streaming platform: CloudFront Signed URLs for episode access (premium users get 24-hour signed URLs). SaaS onboarding: Lambda@Edge checks JWT in viewer-request, returns 401 before the request ever reaches the origin. Multi-region A/B testing: Lambda@Edge assigns users to groups at the edge — no origin needed. CI/CD: GitHub Actions deploys React build to S3, then runs invalidation for /index.html only.
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  Deploy[New Build: bundle.newHash.js] --> S3Up[Upload to S3]
  S3Up --> Inv[Invalidate /index.html only]
  Inv --> Edges[Propagates to 400+ edges in ~45s]
  
  User -->|GET example.com| Edge[Edge PoP]
  Edge --> LAE[Lambda@Edge: viewer-request]
  LAE -->|Check cookie / assign A/B group| Req[Forward to origin]
  Req --> CF[CloudFront cache check]
  CF -->|HIT| Serve[Serve cached asset]
  CF -->|MISS| Origin[Fetch from S3/ALB]
  
  PaidUser -->|GET /course/video.mp4| CFPrivate[CloudFront]
  CFPrivate -->|Signed URL valid?| Video[Serve video]
  CFPrivate -->|Expired| 403[403 Forbidden]
examples.tracerSteps: [{"step":1,"description":"CI/CD pipeline: new React build produces bundle.newHash.js","state":"Old file: bundle.oldHash.js still in S3. New file: bundle.newHash.js uploaded."},{"step":2,"description":"index.html invalidated via create_invalidation","state":"Invalidation ID: ABCDEF. Propagating to all edges (takes ~45s)"},{"step":3,"description":"User requests example.com — Lambda@Edge viewer-request fires","state":"No 'ab_group' cookie. Hash viewer IP → assigned to v2 group."},{"step":4,"description":"Lambda@Edge adds X-AB-Group: v2 header, forwards to origin","state":"CloudFront forwards to S3 with modified headers"},{"step":5,"description":"Edge cache miss for new /assets-v2/bundle.newHash.js","state":"Fetch from S3 origin. Cache-Control: max-age=31536000. Cached at edge."},{"step":6,"description":"Premium user requests /course/episode-1.mp4","state":"Signed URL checked: Policy valid, expiry not passed, signature valid → 200 OK"},{"step":7,"description":"Same user tries the signed URL 2 hours later (expire_seconds=3600)","state":"Signature epoch time < current time → CloudFront returns 403 Forbidden"}]

problems.title: Debug Low Cache Hit Ratio
problems.description: A CloudFront distribution is serving an API at /api/* (POST requests) and a React app at /* (static files). The cache hit ratio is 12% (expected 90%+). Identify 3 likely causes of the low cache hit ratio and propose fixes for each.
constraints: POST requests are never cached by HTTP standard
Query strings and cookies expand the cache key, fragmenting it
Each unique cache key = a separate cached object

examples.input: Cache hit ratio: 12%. Distribution handles both /api/* and /* paths
examples.output: Cause 1: /api/* is POST requests — POST is not cacheable, every request is a miss. Fix: separate behavior for /api/* with CachingDisabled policy. Cause 2: static assets including User-Agent in cache key — millions of unique keys. Fix: remove User-Agent from cache key. Cause 3: index.html has no Cache-Control header — CloudFront uses default TTL=24h but next request is still a 'miss' if directive says no-cache. Fix: set explicit Cache-Control headers on S3 objects
examples.explanation: The /api/* misses are expected and unavoidable — use CachingDisabled. The static asset misses indicate cache key fragmentation

hints: Check CloudFront cache behavior configurations for /api/*
CloudFront console → Reports → Cache Statistics shows hit/miss breakdown by behavior
aws s3 cp index.html s3://bucket/ --cache-control 'no-cache' sets the S3 object metadata
problems.sampleInput: Cache hit ratio: 12%. Distribution handles both /api/* and /* paths
problems.sampleOutput: Cause 1: /api/* is POST requests — POST is not cacheable, every request is a miss. Fix: separate behavior for /api/* with CachingDisabled policy. Cause 2: static assets including User-Agent in cache key — millions of unique keys. Fix: remove User-Agent from cache key. Cause 3: index.html has no Cach

problems.title: Set Up HTTPS with ACM Certificate
problems.description: Configure HTTPS for a CloudFront distribution with custom domain api.example.com. List the steps in order: certificate request, validation, CloudFront configuration, Route 53 record. What region must the ACM certificate be in, and why?
constraints: ACM certificates for CloudFront MUST be in us-east-1 (N. Virginia)
CloudFront is a global service, certificates must be in the global region
DNS validation is preferred over email validation (automated renewal)

examples.output: 1. Request cert in ACM us-east-1 for api.example.com. 2. Add DNS validation CNAME to Route 53 (or auto-validate if hosted zone in same account). 3. Wait for ISSUED status. 4. Add to CloudFront ViewerCertificate.AcmCertificateArn. 5. Add Aliases=['api.example.com']. 6. Route 53 Alias A record → CloudFront domain
examples.explanation: CloudFront is a global service managed from us-east-1 internally — certificates referenced by CloudFront must be in us-east-1 regardless of where your origin is

hints: ACM in us-east-1 only for CloudFront (other services use regional certs)
SslSupportMethod: sni-only (vs vip — expensive dedicated IP)
MinimumProtocolVersion: TLSv1.2_2021 (disables insecure TLS 1.0/1.1)
problems.sampleOutput: 1. Request cert in ACM us-east-1 for api.example.com. 2. Add DNS validation CNAME to Route 53 (or auto-validate if hosted zone in same account). 3. Wait for ISSUED status. 4. Add to CloudFront ViewerCertificate.AcmCertificateArn. 5. Add Aliases=['api.example.com']. 6. Route 53 Alias A record → Cloud

problems.title: Design CloudFront for a Video Streaming Platform
problems.description: Design a CloudFront distribution for a video streaming platform: (1) Free preview (first 2 minutes) of every episode is publicly accessible. (2) Full episodes require an active subscription — generate a signed URL valid for 4 hours. (3) Video files are in S3 (never publicly accessible). (4) Thumbnails are publicly cacheable. What CloudFront configuration, S3 permissions, and URL signing approach would you use?
constraints: S3 bucket must remain private — only CloudFront can access it
Signed URLs must not be shareable after expiry
Thumbnails: long TTL (1 day). Videos: no cache (each request is a unique signed URL)

examples.output: S3 + OAI (blocks direct S3 access). Behaviors: /previews/* → no restriction + long TTL. /episodes/* → CloudFront key group + Signed URL required + CachingDisabled. /thumbs/* → public + max-age=86400. Sign URLs server-side with episode expiry=4h
examples.explanation: CachingDisabled on /episodes/* because each signed URL is unique (different for each user/time) — no point caching

hints: Key Group replaces legacy Origin Access Identity for signed URL enforcement
CachingDisabled policy for signed content — signed URL parameters would fragment cache
Server-side URL signing: your backend generates the signed URL, delivers to client — private key never leaves server
problems.sampleOutput: S3 + OAI (blocks direct S3 access). Behaviors: /previews/* → no restriction + long TTL. /episodes/* → CloudFront key group + Signed URL required + CachingDisabled. /thumbs/* → public + max-age=86400. Sign URLs server-side with episode expiry=4h

topics.title: ALB, NLB, and VPC Peering — Load Balancing and Network Design
topics.description: Application Load Balancer (ALB) distributes HTTP/HTTPS traffic across targets using content-based routing. Network Load Balancer (NLB) handles TCP/UDP at extreme throughput and ultra-low latency. VPC Peering connects two isolated VPCs privately, enabling cross-account and cross-region networking without the public internet.
topics.story: ALB is like a smart hotel switchboard operator — when a call comes in, she listens to what the caller wants ('I'm calling about a reservation') and routes to the right department (reservations). She can also check if the reservations desk is open (health checks) and redirect to another desk if not. NLB is like a raw telephone switch — no listening, no intelligence, just extremely fast connections at the network layer. VPC Peering is like building a private tunnel between two office buildings — staff can walk between buildings on a private corridor (not via public streets).
topics.analogy: ALB = Layer 7 (application layer) load balancer. Rules = routing decisions based on URL path, hostname, headers, or query strings. Target Group = a pool of servers that receive traffic from a rule. Health Check = periodic probe to ensure targets are alive. NLB = Layer 4 (transport layer) load balancer. Operates on TCP/UDP/TLS packets — no HTTP awareness. VPC Peering = bidirectional private network route between two VPCs. No transitive routing — if VPC A peers with VPC B, and VPC B peers with VPC C, A cannot reach C through B.
topics.memoryAnchor: ALB: Layer 7, HTTP/HTTPS/WebSocket, content-based routing, 400ms avg latency added. NLB: Layer 4, TCP/UDP/TLS, static IP per AZ, <100μs latency, handles millions of req/s. Gateway LB (GLWB): Layer 3, for inline traffic inspection (firewalls, IDS). VPC Peering: no transitive routing, supports cross-account and cross-region. Max VPC peer connections: 125 per VPC. CIDR ranges must not overlap.
topics.firstPrinciples: A load balancer solves two problems: distribution (spread load across multiple servers) and availability (if one server dies, route around it). ALB operates at OSI Layer 7 — it terminates HTTP connections, inspects the full request (URL, headers, body), makes a routing decision, then opens a new connection to the target. This adds latency (~0.4ms) but enables powerful content-based routing. NLB operates at Layer 4 — it routes TCP segments based on source/destination IP + port only, without inspecting the payload. NLB preserves the client's source IP (ALB replaces it with the load balancer's IP, passing the original in X-Forwarded-For). VPC Peering creates a 1-1 network route — uses the AWS backbone, never traverses the public internet. The no-transitive-routing rule exists because AWS charges for transit — use Transit Gateway if you need hub-and-spoke networking.
topics.bruteForce: Without a load balancer: one EC2 instance handles all traffic. Single point of failure. When you deploy a new version, downtime. Without VPC Peering: services in different VPCs communicate via the public internet — adds latency, security risk, and data transfer costs (internet egress). Without content-based routing: separate load balancers for each service, each with its own cost and management overhead.
topics.optimizedApproach: ALB listener rules: use path-based routing (/api/users/* → users service, /api/orders/* → orders service) to host multiple microservices behind one ALB — reduces cost and simplifies DNS. Use ALB's built-in sticky sessions (application-based or duration-based cookies) only when necessary — stateless backends scale better. NLB for: gRPC, raw TCP databases, custom TCP protocols, static IP requirements (ALB IPs change). VPC Peering for 1-few connections; Transit Gateway for hub-and-spoke (dozens of VPCs) — TGW is a managed regional router.
topics.whenToUse: ALB: REST/GraphQL APIs, microservices routing, WebSocket, HTTP/2, gRPC (via HTTP/2). NLB: extreme throughput, ultra-low latency, TCP/UDP non-HTTP protocols, static IP requirement (whitelisting), PrivateLink endpoint (NLB is required). VPC Peering: connecting two VPCs (same or different account/region) for private communication. Transit Gateway: when you have 3+ VPCs needing full mesh connectivity.

examples.title: ALB — Listener Rules, Target Groups, and Health Checks
examples.code:
# ── Create ALB ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
aws elbv2 create-load-balancer \
  --name my-app-alb \
  --subnets subnet-public-1a subnet-public-1b \
  --security-groups sg-alb \
  --scheme internet-facing \
  --type application
# Returns: ALB ARN and DNS name (my-app-alb-1234.us-east-1.elb.amazonaws.com)

# ── Target Group: users microservice ─────────────────────────────────
aws elbv2 create-target-group \
  --name users-service-tg \
  --protocol HTTP \
  --port 8080 \
  --vpc-id vpc-xxx \
  --target-type instance \
  --health-check-path /actuator/health \
  --health-check-interval-seconds 30 \
  --healthy-threshold-count 2 \
  --unhealthy-threshold-count 3 \
  --matcher HttpCode=200

# ── Target Group: orders microservice ────────────────────────────────
aws elbv2 create-target-group \
  --name orders-service-tg \
  --protocol HTTP \
  --port 8081 \
  --vpc-id vpc-xxx \
  --health-check-path /health

# ── Register EC2 instances with target groups ─────────────────────────
aws elbv2 register-targets \
  --target-group-arn arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:...:targetgroup/users-service-tg/xxx \
  --targets Id=i-users-1 Id=i-users-2

aws elbv2 register-targets \
  --target-group-arn arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:...:targetgroup/orders-service-tg/xxx \
  --targets Id=i-orders-1

# ── HTTPS Listener with routing rules ────────────────────────────────
aws elbv2 create-listener \
  --load-balancer-arn $ALB_ARN \
  --protocol HTTPS \
  --port 443 \
  --ssl-policy ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06 \
  --certificates CertificateArn=$ACM_CERT_ARN \
  --default-actions Type=fixed-response,FixedResponseConfig='{StatusCode=404,ContentType=application/json,MessageBody="{\"error\":\"Not Found\"}'}'

# Rule 1: /api/users/* → users-service-tg
aws elbv2 create-rule \
  --listener-arn $LISTENER_ARN \
  --priority 10 \
  --conditions Field=path-pattern,Values='/api/users/*' \
  --actions Type=forward,TargetGroupArn=$USERS_TG_ARN

# Rule 2: /api/orders/* → orders-service-tg
aws elbv2 create-rule \
  --listener-arn $LISTENER_ARN \
  --priority 20 \
  --conditions Field=path-pattern,Values='/api/orders/*' \
  --actions Type=forward,TargetGroupArn=$ORDERS_TG_ARN

# Rule 3: Redirect HTTP to HTTPS
aws elbv2 create-listener \
  --load-balancer-arn $ALB_ARN \
  --protocol HTTP \
  --port 80 \
  --default-actions 'Type=redirect,RedirectConfig={Protocol=HTTPS,Port=443,StatusCode=HTTP_301}'

# ── Weighted target groups (canary routing at ALB level) ──────────────
# 90% to v1, 10% to v2 (ALB-level split, not DNS-level)
aws elbv2 create-rule \
  --listener-arn $LISTENER_ARN \
  --priority 5 \
  --conditions Field=path-pattern,Values='/api/new-feature/*' \
  --actions 'Type=forward,ForwardConfig={TargetGroups=[{TargetGroupArn='$V1_TG_ARN',Weight=90},{TargetGroupArn='$V2_TG_ARN',Weight=10}]}'
examples.explanation: ALB listeners define port+protocol combos (443/HTTPS, 80/HTTP). Rules evaluate top-down by priority (lower number = higher priority) against request conditions (path, hostname, header, query string). The default action (lowest priority) handles unmatched requests. Health checks poll each target on a configurable path — only healthy targets receive traffic. Unhealthy targets are removed until they pass N consecutive healthy checks. The HTTP→HTTPS redirect rule at port 80 ensures all traffic is encrypted. Weighted forwarding (multiple target groups in one rule) enables ALB-level canary releases — finer-grained than Route 53 because it's within the same DNS name.
examples.pseudocode:
ALB request routing algorithm:
  Request: POST /api/orders/O99 (from 203.x.x.x)
  
  1. Terminate TLS at ALB (client → ALB: HTTPS, ALB → target: HTTP)
  2. Evaluate listener rules (priority order: 5 → 10 → 20 → default):
     Rule 5: path-pattern /api/new-feature/* → NO MATCH
     Rule 10: path-pattern /api/users/* → NO MATCH
     Rule 20: path-pattern /api/orders/* → MATCH
  3. Forward to orders-service-tg
  4. Target selection: round-robin across healthy targets
     Healthy targets: [i-orders-1]
     → Select i-orders-1
  5. Add headers: X-Forwarded-For: 203.x.x.x, X-Forwarded-Proto: https
  6. Open new HTTP connection to i-orders-1:8081
  7. Forward request, wait for response, return to client
  
  Health check failure:
    i-orders-1 returns 503 on /health (3 consecutive checks)
    → marked unhealthy → removed from routing
    → orders-service-tg: 0 healthy targets
    → ALB returns 503 Service Unavailable
examples.realWorldUse: Spring Boot microservices: single ALB, path-based routing to users-service, orders-service, products-service — each in its own ASG. Blue/green at ALB level: deploy new version to v2 target group, shift weights 90/10 → 0/100. Rolling restart: deregister one instance, update, re-register — ALB drains connections gracefully (connection_draining_timeout). ALB access logs to S3: every request logged with timestamp, source IP, target, latency, response code.
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  Internet -->|HTTPS 443| ALB[Application Load Balancer]
  ALB --> R1{Rule: priority 10}
  R1 -->|/api/users/*| UTG[users-service-tg]
  R1 -->|no match| R2{Rule: priority 20}
  R2 -->|/api/orders/*| OTG[orders-service-tg]
  R2 -->|no match| DEF[Default: 404]
  UTG --> HC1{Health check?}
  HC1 -->|healthy| U1[EC2 users-1]
  HC1 -->|healthy| U2[EC2 users-2]
  HC1 -->|unhealthy| Remove[Removed from rotation]
  HTTP[HTTP:80] -->|redirect| HTTPS[HTTPS:443]
examples.tracerSteps: [{"step":1,"description":"Client: POST https://api.example.com/api/orders/O99","state":"HTTP/1.1 request arrives at ALB port 443"},{"step":2,"description":"ALB terminates TLS, evaluates listener rules","state":"Rule 10 (/api/users/*): no match. Rule 20 (/api/orders/*): MATCH"},{"step":3,"description":"Forward to orders-service-tg — round-robin selection","state":"Healthy targets: [i-orders-1]. Selected: i-orders-1:8081"},{"step":4,"description":"ALB adds X-Forwarded-For: 203.1.2.3, X-Forwarded-Proto: https","state":"New HTTP connection opened to i-orders-1:8081"},{"step":5,"description":"orders-service processes request, returns 201 Created","state":"ALB forwards 201 + body to client. Total ALB overhead: ~0.4ms"},{"step":6,"description":"Health check: i-orders-1 returns 503 on /health (deployment)","state":"After 3 consecutive failures (90s): i-orders-1 marked unhealthy, removed from tg"},{"step":7,"description":"New EC2 instance launched by ASG, registers with orders-service-tg","state":"After 2 successful /health checks (60s): instance added back to rotation"}]

examples.title: NLB vs ALB Trade-Offs and VPC Peering
examples.code:
# ── Network Load Balancer (NLB) — Layer 4, ultra-low latency ─────────
# Use NLB when:
# 1. You need a static IP (NLB assigns one Elastic IP per AZ)
# 2. Protocol is TCP/UDP (not HTTP) — game servers, MQTT, custom protocols
# 3. You need client IP preserved (no X-Forwarded-For — target sees real client IP)
# 4. You're creating an AWS PrivateLink service endpoint
aws elbv2 create-load-balancer \
  --name my-nlb \
  --subnets subnet-1a subnet-1b \
  --type network \
  --scheme internet-facing

# NLB target group: TCP port 5432 (PostgreSQL proxy)
aws elbv2 create-target-group \
  --name postgres-tg \
  --protocol TCP \
  --port 5432 \
  --vpc-id vpc-xxx

# NLB listener: TCP 5432 → postgres-tg
aws elbv2 create-listener \
  --load-balancer-arn $NLB_ARN \
  --protocol TCP \
  --port 5432 \
  --default-actions Type=forward,TargetGroupArn=$PG_TG_ARN

# ── VPC Peering — private connectivity between two VPCs ───────────────
# Scenario: app VPC (10.0.0.0/16) needs to access data VPC (10.1.0.0/16)
# Step 1: Requester VPC creates peering connection request
aws ec2 create-vpc-peering-connection \
  --vpc-id vpc-app \
  --peer-vpc-id vpc-data \
  --peer-region us-east-1  # cross-region peering supported
# Returns: pcx-xxxxxxxx

# Step 2: Accepter VPC owner accepts the peering connection
aws ec2 accept-vpc-peering-connection \
  --vpc-peering-connection-id pcx-xxxxxxxx

# Step 3: Add route to app VPC route table
# (traffic to 10.1.0.0/16 goes via the peering connection)
aws ec2 create-route \
  --route-table-id rtb-app-private \
  --destination-cidr-block 10.1.0.0/16 \
  --vpc-peering-connection-id pcx-xxxxxxxx

# Step 4: Add route to data VPC route table (bidirectional)
aws ec2 create-route \
  --route-table-id rtb-data-private \
  --destination-cidr-block 10.0.0.0/16 \
  --vpc-peering-connection-id pcx-xxxxxxxx

# Step 5: Update Security Groups to allow cross-VPC traffic
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id sg-data-postgres \
  --protocol tcp \
  --port 5432 \
  --cidr 10.0.0.0/16  # Allow app VPC CIDR to reach Postgres

# ── Cross-Account VPC Peering ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Same steps but specify --peer-owner-id for the other account
aws ec2 create-vpc-peering-connection \
  --vpc-id vpc-app \
  --peer-vpc-id vpc-shared-services \
  --peer-owner-id 987654321098  # Other AWS account ID
# The other account must accept via their AWS console or CLI
examples.explanation: NLB vs ALB key differences: NLB operates at TCP level — it doesn't inspect HTTP headers, can't route by path, but achieves sub-millisecond latency and handles millions of connections per second. NLB preserves the client's source IP at the target — the EC2 instance sees the real client IP directly. NLB has static IPs (one per AZ) that can be whitelisted by enterprise firewalls. VPC Peering requires routes in BOTH VPCs' route tables — it's not automatic. Both route tables must have the other VPC's CIDR pointing to the peering connection ID. Security Groups must explicitly allow the cross-VPC traffic. No transitive routing: A↔B and B↔C doesn't enable A↔C.
examples.pseudocode:
VPC Peering no-transitive routing:
  VPC-A (10.0.0.0/16) ←→ VPC-B (10.1.0.0/16)
  VPC-B (10.1.0.0/16) ←→ VPC-C (10.2.0.0/16)
  
  EC2 in VPC-A tries to reach EC2 in VPC-C (10.2.1.5):
    1. Check VPC-A route table: 10.2.0.0/16 → ?
    2. No route (peering only created A↔B, not A↔C)
    3. Packet: no route → DROPPED
  
  To allow A↔C:
    Option 1: Create VPC-A ↔ VPC-C peering connection
    Option 2: Use Transit Gateway (TGW) — managed hub:
      TGW attachment in each VPC
      TGW route table: A, B, C all peer through TGW
      A can reach C via TGW (transitive routing supported)
  
  NLB source IP preservation:
    Client: 203.1.2.3 → NLB → EC2 target
    EC2 sees source IP: 203.1.2.3 (real client)
    
    ALB: Client: 203.1.2.3 → ALB → EC2 target
    EC2 sees source IP: ALB private IP (10.0.1.x)
    Real client IP in: X-Forwarded-For: 203.1.2.3 header
examples.realWorldUse: NLB for: database connection pooler (PgBouncer) behind static IP whitelisted by client firewalls. Game server UDP traffic routing. gRPC services (HTTP/2 works on both, but NLB adds less overhead). VPC Peering: prod app VPC peers with shared-services VPC (Active Directory, monitoring). Cross-account: dev account peers with a shared sandbox data VPC for integration testing. Data pipeline: analytics VPC peers with transactional VPC to query the database directly without going through the public internet.
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  subgraph ALB_Flow
    Client1[Client] -->|HTTPS: POST /api/orders| ALB1[ALB Layer 7]
    ALB1 -->|path routing| TG1[Target Group: orders]
    ALB1 -->|path routing| TG2[Target Group: users]
  end
  subgraph NLB_Flow
    Client2[Client] -->|TCP:5432| NLB1[NLB Layer 4]
    NLB1 -->|preserves client IP| PG[PostgreSQL]
  end
  subgraph VPC_Peering
    AppVPC[App VPC 10.0.0.0/16] <-->|pcx-xxx| DataVPC[Data VPC 10.1.0.0/16]
    DataVPC <-->|pcx-yyy| SharedVPC[Shared Services 10.2.0.0/16]
    AppVPC -->|NO ROUTE no transitive| SharedVPC
  end
examples.tracerSteps: [{"step":1,"description":"Enterprise client needs to connect to PostgreSQL on port 5432 via whitelisted IP","state":"NLB created with Elastic IP: 52.1.2.3 (static). Client whitelists 52.1.2.3."},{"step":2,"description":"Client connects TCP to 52.1.2.3:5432","state":"NLB receives TCP SYN, selects healthy target (PgBouncer on i-db-proxy-1)"},{"step":3,"description":"NLB forwards TCP segment to i-db-proxy-1:5432","state":"Source IP preserved: i-db-proxy-1 sees client's real IP 203.x.x.x in TCP connection"},{"step":4,"description":"VPC Peering: App VPC (10.0.0.0/16) needs to call internal API in Data VPC (10.1.0.0/16)","state":"Peering connection pcx-abc created and accepted"},{"step":5,"description":"Routes added: App VPC rtb: 10.1.0.0/16 → pcx-abc | Data VPC rtb: 10.0.0.0/16 → pcx-abc","state":"EC2 in App VPC (10.0.1.5) sends packet to Data VPC (10.1.2.10)"},{"step":6,"description":"Packet traverses peering connection via AWS backbone (no public internet)","state":"Latency: ~0.3ms (same-region peering). Security Group sg-data allows 10.0.0.0/16 on port 8080"},{"step":7,"description":"App VPC tries to reach Shared Services VPC (10.2.0.0/16) via Data VPC","state":"NO ROUTE in App VPC route table for 10.2.0.0/16 → packet dropped. Transitive routing not supported."}]

problems.title: ALB Rule Priority Design
problems.description: Design ALB listener rules for an application with these routing requirements: (1) GET /health → return 200 OK immediately without hitting backend (used by external monitoring). (2) /admin/* → forward to admin-service target group. (3) /api/* → forward to api-service target group. (4) Everything else → forward to frontend-service target group. Assign priorities correctly.
constraints: Lower priority number = evaluated first
More specific rules should have lower priority numbers (evaluated first)
Fixed-response action returns a response without hitting any backend

examples.input: 4 routing rules, /health should be fastest (no backend call)
examples.output: Priority 1: /health → fixed-response 200. Priority 10: /admin/* → admin-tg. Priority 20: /api/* → api-tg. Default: frontend-tg (no rule needed — it's the default action)
examples.explanation: Fixed-response at priority 1 for /health: health check probes hit this 30× per minute per instance — keeping them away from the backend reduces unnecessary load

hints: Default action (no rule) handles everything not matched by a rule
fixed-response content-type must be specified: application/json or text/plain
Priority gaps (1, 10, 20 instead of 1, 2, 3) leave room to insert new rules later
problems.sampleInput: 4 routing rules, /health should be fastest (no backend call)
problems.sampleOutput: Priority 1: /health → fixed-response 200. Priority 10: /admin/* → admin-tg. Priority 20: /api/* → api-tg. Default: frontend-tg (no rule needed — it's the default action)

problems.title: Choose ALB vs NLB
problems.description: For each scenario, choose ALB or NLB and give the decisive reason: (1) REST API with JWT authentication. (2) Real-time multiplayer game server using UDP. (3) gRPC microservices (HTTP/2 transport). (4) Database proxy that enterprise clients whitelist by IP. (5) WebSocket chat application.
constraints: ALB: HTTP/HTTPS/WebSocket, Layer 7, content-based routing, dynamic IPs
NLB: TCP/UDP/TLS, Layer 4, static IPs, sub-millisecond latency

examples.input: Game server using UDP for position updates
examples.output: NLB — UDP is Layer 4. ALB only supports HTTP/HTTPS/WebSocket. NLB handles UDP natively.
examples.explanation: ALB cannot inspect UDP packets — it's purely an HTTP-aware load balancer

hints: If the protocol is not HTTP/HTTPS/WebSocket → NLB
If static IP is required for firewall whitelisting → NLB
WebSocket: ALB supports it natively (HTTP Upgrade header) → ALB is fine
problems.sampleInput: Game server using UDP for position updates
problems.sampleOutput: NLB — UDP is Layer 4. ALB only supports HTTP/HTTPS/WebSocket. NLB handles UDP natively.

problems.title: Debug VPC Peering Connectivity Issue
problems.description: You set up VPC Peering between App VPC (10.0.0.0/16) and DB VPC (10.1.0.0/16). The peering connection is ACTIVE. EC2 in App VPC cannot reach RDS in DB VPC on port 5432. List 3 things to check in order, explaining what misconfiguration each would reveal.
constraints: Peering connection status ACTIVE means the connection is established
Route tables and security groups are separate from the peering connection itself
Security groups can reference CIDR ranges or other security group IDs

examples.input: Peering ACTIVE but no connectivity. App VPC: 10.0.0.0/16, DB VPC: 10.1.0.0/16
examples.output: Check 1: App VPC route table — does it have 10.1.0.0/16 → pcx-xxx? Check 2: DB VPC route table — does it have 10.0.0.0/16 → pcx-xxx? Check 3: RDS security group — does it allow TCP:5432 from 10.0.0.0/16 (or from EC2 security group ID)?
examples.explanation: Peering ACTIVE is just the handshake. Routes and security groups are independent layers that must also be configured

hints: aws ec2 describe-route-tables shows all routes
aws ec2 describe-security-groups shows inbound rules
Use VPC Reachability Analyzer to trace the exact point of failure
problems.sampleInput: Peering ACTIVE but no connectivity. App VPC: 10.0.0.0/16, DB VPC: 10.1.0.0/16
problems.sampleOutput: Check 1: App VPC route table — does it have 10.1.0.0/16 → pcx-xxx? Check 2: DB VPC route table — does it have 10.0.0.0/16 → pcx-xxx? Check 3: RDS security group — does it allow TCP:5432 from 10.0.0.0/16 (or from EC2 security group ID)?

problems.title: ALB Connection Draining for Zero-Downtime Deployments
problems.description: You need to deploy a new version of a service running behind an ALB without dropping any in-flight requests. Describe the deployment steps using ALB target group deregistration and connection draining. What is the deregistration_delay setting, and what happens to requests during the draining period?
constraints: Connection draining (deregistration_delay): 0-3600s, default 300s
During drain: no new requests routed to deregistering target
During drain: existing in-flight requests complete normally

examples.output: 1. Deregister instance-1 from target group. 2. ALB stops sending new requests to instance-1 (deregistration_delay=30s). 3. In-flight requests on instance-1 complete. 4. After drain: instance-1 fully deregistered. 5. Stop instance-1, deploy v2, restart. 6. Register new v2 instance. 7. Health checks pass. 8. Repeat for instance-2.
examples.explanation: deregistration_delay=300s (default) is too long for most apps — set it to ~30s (your p99 request time) to speed up deployments without dropping requests

hints: aws elbv2 modify-target-group-attributes --deregistration-delay.timeout-seconds 30
During draining: ALB sends to remaining healthy targets only. If only 1 instance left: it handles 100% of traffic
EC2 Auto Scaling respects connection draining before terminating instances
problems.sampleOutput: 1. Deregister instance-1 from target group. 2. ALB stops sending new requests to instance-1 (deregistration_delay=30s). 3. In-flight requests on instance-1 complete. 4. After drain: instance-1 fully deregistered. 5. Stop instance-1, deploy v2, restart. 6. Register new v2 instance. 7. Health checks p
